citation: Vermeer K. Food and populations of Surf Scoters in British Columbia. Wildfowl 32:107-116.
evidence_contribution: Diet and population observations; herring eggs became the main food for about three spring weeks.
project_relevance: Early direct evidence for a short species-relevant resource window.
analysis_consequence: Supports event timing; no new covariate.

citation: Haegele CW. Seabird predation of Pacific Herring spawn in British Columbia. Canadian Field-Naturalist.
evidence_contribution: Quantified avian consumption of spawn and identified principal bird predators.
project_relevance: Mechanistic and quantitative foundation.
analysis_consequence: Interpret local response and predation only.

citation: Sullivan TM; Butler RW; Boyd WS. Seasonal distribution of waterbirds in relation to spawning Pacific Herring in the Strait of Georgia. Canadian Field-Naturalist 116:366-370.
evidence_contribution: Dedicated shoreline counts showed a large numerical aggregation during spawn.
project_relevance: Closest precedent for local aggregation and exposed-versus-baseline counts.

citation: Rodway MS; Cooke F. Seasonal changes in diet of wintering Harlequin Ducks at a herring spawning site. Journal of Field Ornithology 73:363-371.
evidence_contribution: Diet evidence links Harlequin Duck use directly to herring spawn.
project_relevance: Supports biological mechanism.

citation: Rodway MS et al. Aggregative response of Harlequin Ducks to herring spawning in the Strait of Georgia. Canadian Journal of Zoology 81:504-514.
evidence_contribution: Marked birds and counts showed strong aggregation and redistribution.
project_relevance: Supports aggregation redistribution distance and heterogeneity among spawn sites.

citation: Lewis TL; Esler D; Boyd WS. Foraging behaviors of Surf Scoters and White-winged Scoters during spawning of Pacific Herring. Condor 109:216-222.
evidence_contribution: Scoters changed dive and foraging behavior while using roe.
project_relevance: Supports mechanism and short event window.

citation: Lok EK; Kirk M; Esler D; Boyd WS. Movements of pre-migratory Surf and White-winged Scoters in response to Pacific Herring spawning. Waterbirds 31:385-393.
evidence_contribution: Telemetry documented movement beyond ordinary winter ranges toward spawn.
project_relevance: Direct precedent for redistribution and distance-response models.

citation: Lok EK et al. Spatiotemporal associations between Pacific Herring spawn and Surf Scoter spring migration. MEPS 457:139-150.
evidence_contribution: Telemetry and aerial surveys showed migratory tracking of spawn phenology.
project_relevance: Supports phenology event timing and resource-wave interpretation.

citation: Kelly JP; Rothenbach K; Weathers WW. Responses of wintering waterbirds to Pacific Herring spawns. MEPS 597.
evidence_contribution: Lagged spawning biomass improved waterbird abundance forecasts in Tomales Bay.
project_relevance: Supports interannual and lagged sensitivity interpretation.

citation: Stewart H. Avian and shallow water community response to Pacific Herring spawn events. WWU thesis.
evidence_contribution: Community counts and exclusion work at Cherry Point.
project_relevance: Broadens regional species and egg-survival context.

citation: Thomas AC et al. Harbor seal foraging response to a seasonal resource pulse spawning Pacific Herring. MEPS 441:225-239.
evidence_contribution: Predator response was not uniformly positive.
project_relevance: Counterexample against assuming every predator aggregates.

citation: Fox CH et al. Novel species interactions: American black bears respond to Pacific Herring spawn. BMC Ecology.
evidence_contribution: Beach activity and diet increased with egg availability.
project_relevance: Shows cross-ecosystem reach of the pulse.

citation: Fox CH et al. Pacific Herring spawn events influence nearshore subtidal and intertidal species. MEPS.
evidence_contribution: Stable-isotope evidence traced herring-derived nutrients through nearshore taxa.
project_relevance: Supports ecosystem-pulse framing.

citation: Haegele CW; Schweigert JF. Distribution and characteristics of herring spawning grounds and description of spawning behaviour. CJFAS.
evidence_contribution: Foundational synthesis of spawn habitat timing depth and substrate.
project_relevance: Defines the exposure process.

citation: Hay DE; Kronlund AR. Factors affecting the distribution abundance and measurement of Pacific Herring spawn. CJFAS.
evidence_contribution: Analyzed historical records and spawn measurement.
project_relevance: Observed spawn reflects ecology and survey process.

citation: Hay DE. Tidal influence on spawning time of Pacific Herring. CJFAS.
evidence_contribution: Examined fine-scale timing relative to tides.
project_relevance: Supports timing interpretation.
analysis_consequence: Does not require tide as a primary covariate.

citation: Hay DE; McCarter PB. Distribution and timing of herring spawning in British Columbia. DFO Research Document 99/14.
evidence_contribution: Long-run spatial and temporal variation across BC regions.
project_relevance: Supports regional heterogeneity and long-window sensitivity.

citation: Hay DE et al. Spatial diversity of Pacific Herring spawning areas. ICES Journal of Marine Science 66:1662-1666.
evidence_contribution: Quantified change in spatial footprint and diversity across BC.
project_relevance: Supports shoreline geometry and partial pooling.

citation: Shelton AO et al. Habitat limitation and spatial variation in Pacific Herring egg survival. MEPS.
evidence_contribution: Egg survival varied spatially and with habitat conditions.
project_relevance: Equal extent need not imply equal availability.

citation: Qi L et al. Satellite remote sensing of Herring spawning events. Geophysical Research Letters 48:e2020GL092126.
evidence_contribution: Some milt plumes and extents can be detected from satellite imagery.
project_relevance: Supports independent event validation.
analysis_consequence: Optional validation; not primary response data.

citation: McGowan DW et al. Multi-decadal shifts in Pacific Herring spawning in Prince William Sound. CJFAS.
evidence_contribution: Documented changes in where and when spawning occurred.
project_relevance: Supports nonstationary exposure geography and phenology.

citation: Schweigert JF. Synopsis of the development of dive surveys for Pacific Herring spawn in BC. DFO.
evidence_contribution: Documents modern dive-survey observation process.
project_relevance: Supports survey-era boundary.

citation: Grinnell MH et al. Calculating the spawn index for Pacific Herring in British Columbia. Technical Report 3489.
evidence_contribution: Current official equations observation types aggregation uncertainties and caveats.
project_relevance: Primary exposure-method citation.

citation: Rooper CN et al. Evaluating factors affecting distribution and timing of Pacific Herring spawn in BC. MEPS 741:251-269.
evidence_contribution: Modern 1988-2018 dive-era spatial and temporal model.
project_relevance: Supports regional and phenological heterogeneity.

citation: Schweigert JF et al. Biological synopsis of Pacific Herring in support of marine risk assessment. DFO.
evidence_contribution: Official life-history stock-structure and ecology synthesis.
project_relevance: Authoritative background.

citation: Government of Canada. Pacific Herring Spawn Index Data.
evidence_contribution: Official relative-index definition and availability fields.
project_relevance: Dataset citation and exposure guardrail.

citation: Sullivan BL et al. eBird: a citizen-based bird observation network in the biological sciences. Biological Conservation 142:2282-2292.
evidence_contribution: Describes checklist system validation and applications.
project_relevance: Foundational data-source citation.

citation: Fink D et al. Spatiotemporal exploratory models for broad-scale survey data. Ecological Applications 20:2131-2147.
evidence_contribution: Flexible spatiotemporal modeling framework.
project_relevance: Precedent for dynamic distributions.

citation: Johnston A et al. Estimates of observer expertise improve species distributions from citizen science data. Methods in Ecology and Evolution 9:88-97.
evidence_contribution: Observer expertise affected detectability and prediction.
project_relevance: Justifies observer heterogeneity.

citation: Kelling S et al. Using semistructured surveys to improve citizen science data for monitoring biodiversity. BioScience 69:170-179.
evidence_contribution: Observation-process metadata make semistructured checklists analytically useful.
project_relevance: Supports complete checklists rather than presence-only records.

citation: Fink D et al. Modeling avian full annual cycle distribution and population trends with citizen science data. Ecological Applications 30:e02056.
evidence_contribution: Large-scale dynamic distribution and trend estimation.
project_relevance: Demonstrates capability at a different scale.

citation: Robinson OJ et al. Integrating citizen science data with expert surveys increases accuracy and spatial extent of species distribution models. Diversity and Distributions.
evidence_contribution: Joint models improved inference when observation processes were retained.
project_relevance: Supports separate-stream validation not naive pooling.

citation: Johnston A et al. Analytical guidelines to increase the value of community science data. Diversity and Distributions 27:1265-1277.
evidence_contribution: Complete checklists effort covariates spatial balancing and validation improved estimates.
project_relevance: Primary analytical guidance for EBD/SED.

citation: Tang B; Clark JS; Gelfand AE. Modeling spatially biased citizen science effort through the eBird database. Environmental and Ecological Statistics 28:609-630.
evidence_contribution: Models visited locations and effort.
project_relevance: Warns about preferential visits to conspicuous spawn events.

citation: Schindler AR et al. Joint analysis of structured and semi-structured community science data. Scientific Reports 12:20289.
evidence_contribution: Joint analysis improved abundance precision but not trends uniformly.
project_relevance: Integration gains are estimand-specific.

citation: Fink D et al. A double machine learning trend model for citizen science data. Methods in Ecology and Evolution 14:2435-2448.
evidence_contribution: Addresses interannual confounding in flexible sampling.
project_relevance: Useful trend context and diagnostic precedent.

citation: Hochachka WM; Ruiz-Gutierrez V; Johnston A. Considerations for fitting occupancy models to data from eBird and similar volunteer-collected data. Ornithology 140:ukad035.
evidence_contribution: Pseudo-repeats can bias occupancy and added single visits can worsen bias when detection is low.
project_relevance: Defines limits of occupancy interpretation.
analysis_consequence: Keep the primary estimand as checklist encounter.

citation: Stuber EF et al. Validating the performance of eBird against targeted avian monitoring efforts. Biological Conservation 270:109556.
evidence_contribution: Compares eBird estimates with targeted monitoring.
project_relevance: Performance depends on species and decision context.
analysis_consequence: Use external validation without assuming equivalence.

citation: Grade AM et al. Sampling bias in community science: a case study with eBird. PLOS ONE 17:e0277223.
evidence_contribution: Uneven participation and site access can induce sample-selection bias.
project_relevance: Motivates a response-blind support audit.
analysis_consequence: Report checklist and unique-observer support by frozen exposure cells.

citation: Feng MLE et al. Comparing the reliability of relative bird abundance indices from standardized surveys and community science. PLOS ONE 16:e0257226.
evidence_contribution: eBird relative abundance reliability varies by species and setting.
project_relevance: Reinforces relative-index interpretation.

citation: Robinson OJ et al. Same view through a different lens: comparing population trends using eBird and the Breeding Bird Survey. Ornithological Applications 128:duaf077.
evidence_contribution: Large cross-survey comparison shows agreement is not automatic.
project_relevance: Structured surveys are external benchmarks rather than duplicate confirmation.
analysis_consequence: Interpret cross-source agreement by species and region.

citation: Bower JL. Changes in marine bird abundance in the Salish Sea: 1975 to 2007. Marine Ornithology 37:9-17.
evidence_contribution: Long-run regional marine-bird change.
project_relevance: Contextualizes event responses against older baselines.

citation: Crewe T et al. Coastal waterbird population trends in the Strait of Georgia 1999-2011. British Columbia Birds 22:8-39.
evidence_contribution: Structured BCCWS route trends and power analysis.
project_relevance: Regional benchmark.
analysis_consequence: Use as literature; deduplicate before raw integration.

citation: Ethier D et al. Twenty years of coastal waterbird trends in British Columbia. Avian Conservation and Ecology 15(2):20.
evidence_contribution: Species- and region-specific coastal trends.
project_relevance: Separates event response from long-run redistribution.

citation: de Zwaan DR et al. Occupancy trends of overwintering coastal waterbird communities. Global Change Biology 30:e17178.
evidence_contribution: Guild-specific redistribution and shifting protected-area reliance.
project_relevance: Supports region-specific baselines and no forced coastwide effect.

citation: Ethier D et al. Modeling coastal waterbird trends in transboundary Salish Sea waters. MEPS.
evidence_contribution: Integrates BC and Washington structured surveys at multiple scales.
project_relevance: Current regional benchmark.

citation: Hart KA et al. Seabird responses to altered marine vessel activity during the COVID-19 anthropause. Science of the Total Environment 1015:181456.
evidence_contribution: Combined AIS and eBird for 35 seabird species at 44 BC sites.
project_relevance: Closest recent BC coastal eBird application.
analysis_consequence: Supports feasibility; warns about vessel heterogeneity.

citation: Yang LH et al. What can we learn from resource pulses? Ecology 89:621-634.
evidence_contribution: Synthesizes behavioral numerical and community responses.
project_relevance: Vocabulary for aggregation versus demography.

citation: Yang LH et al. A meta-analysis of resource pulse-consumer interactions. Ecological Monographs 80:125-151.
evidence_contribution: Response magnitude lag and duration vary among pulses and consumers.
project_relevance: Supports species heterogeneity and short aquatic lags.

citation: Cury PM et al. Global seabird response to forage fish depletion. Science 334:1703-1706.
evidence_contribution: Seabird breeding success became less reliable below prey thresholds across systems.
project_relevance: Broad forage-fish importance.

citation: Armstrong JB et al. Resource waves: phenological diversity enhances foraging opportunities for mobile consumers. Ecology 97:1099-1112.
evidence_contribution: Formalizes mobile tracking of shifting resource phenology.
project_relevance: Conceptual bridge to the silver wave.

citation: Abrahms B et al. Emerging perspectives on resource tracking and animal movement ecology. Trends in Ecology and Evolution.
evidence_contribution: Synthesizes dynamic resource tracking.
project_relevance: Modern redistribution framing.

citation: Birds Canada. NatureCounts and eBird.
evidence_contribution: NatureCounts users can automatically export app checklists to eBird.
project_relevance: Establishes a duplicate-record pathway.
analysis_consequence: Require checklist crosswalk before raw BCCWS integration.

citation: Birds Canada. NatureCounts dataset catalogue: British Columbia Coastal Waterbird Survey.
evidence_contribution: Confirms BCCWS as a separately maintained structured checklist dataset.
project_relevance: Potential validation stream with overlap risk.
